enrichment.short_description: Multiplier is a global employment platform and Employer of Record (EOR) provider that lets companies hire, pay, and manage full-time employees and contractors in 150+ countries without opening local legal entities.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (per-employee or per-contractor monthly fee) with custom Enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: EOR from $400/month per employee; Contractor Management from $40/month per contractor
enrichment.company_size: 1,000-plus employees (reported over 1,200 across Singapore, New York, and India)
enrichment.seo_title: Multiplier Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Multiplier review for 2026 covering EOR pricing, global payroll, contractor management, key features, pros and cons, and the best alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Multiplier is a global employment platform, also known as an Employer of Record (EOR) provider, that lets companies hire, pay, and manage full-time employees and independent contractors in more than 150 countries without setting up local legal entities. It was founded in 2020 in Singapore by Sagar Khatri, Amritpal Singh, and Vamsi Krishna. The company raised a $4 million seed round before launch and closed a $60 million Series B in 2022 led by Peak XV Partners (formerly Sequoia Capital India) and Tiger Global, bringing total funding to roughly $77 million. Multiplier is headquartered in Singapore with additional offices in New York and India, and employs more than 1,200 people. Multiplier operates through a combination of its own owned legal entities, more than 40 across six continents, and local infrastructure partners, allowing it to act as the legal employer of record on behalf of client companies. This lets a business in one country legally employ workers, run payroll, administer statutory benefits, and manage tax withholding in another country without incorporating a subsidiary there. The platform bundles contract generation, compliance monitoring, payroll processing, benefits administration, visa and immigration support, and equity management into a single dashboard and API. Core products include Employer of Record, Global Payroll for owned entities, Contractor Management with multi-currency invoicing and worker classification checks, background checks, and expense management. Multiplier reports processing around 2 billion dollars in wages annually and serving more than 2,700 companies. Pricing follows a flat, per-worker, per-month subscription model rather than a percentage of salary. EOR plans start at approximately 400 dollars per employee per month, covering the compliance and legal-employer service, while local payroll taxes and statutory contributions are billed separately based on the country. Contractor Management starts at 40 dollars per active contractor per month, covering contract generation, compliance checks, and cross-border payments; inactive contractors are not billed. Global Payroll and Enterprise tiers use custom quotes based on entity count and headcount. Multiplier targets HR, People, and Talent teams at startups through enterprise companies that are scaling international hiring and want to avoid the time and cost of opening foreign subsidiaries. Typical customers are remote-first technology companies, agencies, and larger enterprises entering new markets. Its main competitors are Deel, Remote, Papaya Global, Rippling, Globalization Partners (G-P), and Oyster HR. Multiplier differentiates itself with a large base of directly owned entities, which reduces reliance on third-party local partners for compliance, a single platform spanning EOR, global payroll, and contractor management, transparent flat per-employee pricing versus percentage-of-salary billing used by some rivals, and particularly strong coverage across Asia-Pacific markets alongside its Europe and Americas footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Multiplier is a SaaS platform that functions as an Employer of Record, letting companies legally hire full-time staff and contractors in more than 150 countries without establishing a local subsidiary in each market.

Founded in 2020 in Singapore, the company has grown to over 1,200 employees and more than 2,700 customers, backed by roughly 77 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Peak XV Partners and Tiger Global.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Multiplier's core offering is EOR hiring across 150-plus countries, supported by more than 40 directly owned legal entities plus local infrastructure partners, which handles contracts, statutory benefits, tax withholding, and termination compliance.

Beyond EOR, the platform offers Global Payroll for owned entities, Contractor Management with multi-currency invoicing, background checks, expense management, and equity management, all accessible from one dashboard and API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Multiplier uses flat, per-worker monthly pricing rather than a percentage of salary. EOR plans start around 400 dollars per employee per month for the compliance and employer-of-record service, with local statutory costs billed on top based on jurisdiction.
Contractor Management starts at 40 dollars per active contractor per month, and companies needing multiple products or higher volume typically move to a custom Enterprise quote.

features.title: Employer of Record (EOR)
features.description: Legally employs workers in 150-plus countries on a client's behalf, handling local contracts, statutory benefits, and compliance.

features.title: Global Payroll
features.description: Runs payroll for employees in countries where Multiplier holds an owned entity.

features.title: Contractor Management
features.description: Generates compliant contracts and processes multi-currency payments for independent contractors.

features.title: Compliance monitoring
features.description: Tracks changing local labor law, tax, and statutory benefit requirements across supported countries.

features.title: Benefits administration
features.description: Provides access to local health insurance, pension, and statutory benefit programs for employees.

features.title: Visa and immigration support
features.description: Assists with work permit and visa sponsorship for relocating or international hires.

features.title: Equity management
features.description: Helps companies grant and administer stock options to globally distributed employees.

features.title: Background checks
features.description: Offers pre-employment screening as an add-on service during onboarding.

pros: Large base of directly owned entities reduces reliance on third-party local partners
Flat per-employee pricing is more predictable than percentage-of-salary EOR models
Broad country coverage, including strong presence in Asia-Pacific markets
Single platform combines EOR, payroll, and contractor management
Self-service dashboard for onboarding, payroll, and compliance tracking
cons: EOR base fee excludes local statutory costs, which can raise the effective total cost
Enterprise and Global Payroll pricing is not published and requires a sales quote
Newer entrant compared to some competitors with longer track records in specific regions
Support quality and response times can vary by country according to some reviews
Contractor fee is billed per active contract, which can add up for companies with many part-time contractors

faqs.question: What does Multiplier do?
faqs.answer: Multiplier is an Employer of Record platform that lets companies hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors in over 150 countries without opening a local legal entity.

faqs.question: How much does Multiplier cost?
faqs.answer: EOR services start at around 400 dollars per employee per month, and Contractor Management starts at 40 dollars per active contractor per month, with Global Payroll and Enterprise pricing quoted individually.

faqs.question: When was Multiplier founded?
faqs.answer: Multiplier was founded in 2020 in Singapore by Sagar Khatri, Amritpal Singh, and Vamsi Krishna.

faqs.question: Where is Multiplier headquartered?
faqs.answer: Multiplier is headquartered in Singapore, with additional offices in New York and India.

faqs.question: How many countries does Multiplier support?
faqs.answer: Multiplier supports hiring in more than 150 countries through a combination of owned entities and local partner infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who are Multiplier's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Multiplier competes primarily with Deel, Remote, Papaya Global, Rippling, Globalization Partners (G-P), and Oyster HR.

enrichment.short_description: Mumble is a free, open-source, low-latency voice chat application originally built for gamers that offers high quality encrypted real-time audio communication.
enrichment.seo_title: Mumble Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mumble review for 2026 covering this free, open-source voice chat app: low-latency audio, encryption, self-hosting, features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mumble is a free and open-source, low-latency voice chat application originally created for gamers who need clear, real-time team communication during play. It was created by Norwegian developer Thorvald Natvig and first released on September 2, 2005, making it one of the earliest applications to achieve genuinely low-latency VoIP communication, years before mainstream competitors matched that performance. Mumble has no company behind it; it is maintained by the volunteer-driven Mumble VoIP Team and a community of open-source contributors on GitHub, and is licensed under the permissive BSD-3-Clause license. Because it is open source and self-hosted or community-hosted, there is no commercial pricing, funding history, or acquisition to report. Architecturally, Mumble uses a client-server model: users connect to a server component (historically called Murmur, now integrated into the main Mumble server binary) that manages a hierarchical tree of text and voice channels, allowing communities and gaming clans to organize conversations by topic, team, or game lobby. Communication uses the Opus audio codec for efficient, low-latency compression, with earlier support for CELT and Speex. Security is built in: control channels use TLS, and voice data has been protected first with AES in OCB mode and later upgraded to ECDHE key exchange with AES-GCM for forward secrecy. Authentication relies primarily on public-key certificates rather than passwords, and administrators can lock servers down with access control lists. Mumble is written in C++ on top of the Qt framework and is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS, with servers commonly self-hosted by individuals, gaming communities, or rented from third-party Mumble server hosting providers. Distinctive features include an in-game overlay that displays who is talking over fullscreen games, positional audio that spatially places teammates' voices based on their in-game location (supported natively by titles like Team Fortress 2 and Guild Wars 2), push-to-talk and voice-activity detection, and remote server administration via the ZeroC Ice interface. Mumble's core audience is PC gamers, esports teams, and open-source and privacy-conscious communities who want dedicated, low-latency voice chat without relying on a third-party company's servers or data practices. Its main competitors and alternatives include Discord, TeamSpeak, Ventrilo (legacy), and Discord-adjacent open-source projects. Mumble differentiates itself through its fully open-source codebase, self-hosting model that keeps data under the user's control, historically superior audio latency, and lack of any subscription, advertising, or data-monetization business model, in contrast to commercial competitors like Discord and TeamSpeak.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mumble is a free, open-source voice chat application built to give gamers and teams near-instant, high-quality audio communication over the internet, with all communication encrypted.
Created by Thorvald Natvig and first released in 2005, Mumble is maintained by a volunteer community rather than a company, and is distributed under the BSD-3-Clause open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mumble uses a client-server architecture with a hierarchical channel system, the Opus audio codec for low-latency compression, and TLS plus AES-GCM encryption for secure voice and control traffic.

Notable extras include an in-game overlay for fullscreen games, positional audio that spatially places teammates based on in-game location, and public-key certificate authentication instead of passwords.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mumble itself is entirely free and open source; there are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees for the client or server software.
Users typically self-host a Mumble server on their own hardware or a VPS, or rent one from third-party Mumble hosting providers, which is the only recurring cost some users incur.

features.title: Low-latency voice chat
features.description: Uses the Opus codec to deliver near real-time audio quality suited to fast-paced gaming and team coordination.

features.title: Client-server channel system
features.description: Organizes users into a hierarchical tree of text and voice channels on a self-hosted or rented server.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Protects control and voice traffic using TLS and AES-GCM with public-key certificate authentication.

features.title: In-game overlay
features.description: Shows who is currently speaking directly over fullscreen games without alt-tabbing.

features.title: Positional audio
features.description: Spatially places teammates' voices based on their location in supported games such as Team Fortress 2.

features.title: Cross-platform clients
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Lets communities run their own Mumble server for full control over data and uptime.

features.title: Remote administration
features.description: Supports remote server management through the ZeroC Ice interface.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscriptions or ads
Historically excellent, low audio latency well suited to competitive gaming
Strong encryption and certificate-based authentication
Full self-hosting control over data and server uptime
Lightweight client that runs well on older hardware
cons: Dated interface compared to modern chat apps like Discord
Requires self-hosting or a paid third-party host, unlike free-to-join platforms
Smaller ecosystem of integrations, bots, and community features than Discord
Initial server setup and configuration can be technical for non-technical users
No built-in text-first community features like servers with public discovery

faqs.question: Is Mumble free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mumble is completely free and open source under the BSD-3-Clause license, though users need a server to connect to.

faqs.question: Who created Mumble?
faqs.answer: Mumble was created by Norwegian developer Thorvald Natvig and first released in September 2005.

faqs.question: Does Mumble require a company account?
faqs.answer: No, Mumble has no central company account system; users connect directly to whichever server address they are given, self-hosted or rented.

faqs.question: What platforms does Mumble support?
faqs.answer: Mumble has clients for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS.

faqs.question: How is Mumble different from Discord?
faqs.answer: Mumble is open source, self-hosted, and focused purely on low-latency voice, while Discord is a commercial, centrally hosted platform with broader chat, community, and bot features.

faqs.question: Is Mumble voice chat encrypted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mumble encrypts control channels with TLS and voice data with AES-GCM, and relies on public-key certificates for authentication.

use_cases.title: Competitive gaming voice comms
use_cases.description: A gaming clan needs extremely low-latency, reliable voice chat during matches without a third party controlling their server.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted community voice server
use_cases.description: A privacy-conscious community wants to run its own voice server instead of depending on a commercial platform's infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Lightweight team coordination
use_cases.description: A small team wants simple, low-resource voice channels for coordination without the overhead of a full chat platform.

features: Open-source client for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS
Unlimited channels and users limited only by server hardware

enrichment.short_description: Munch (GetMunch) is an AI-powered video repurposing platform that automatically turns long-form video into short, captioned clips optimized for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts.
enrichment.seo_title: Munch Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Munch (GetMunch) review for 2026: AI video repurposing features, pricing plans, pros and cons, and the best alternatives for short-form clip creation.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Munch, marketed at getmunch.com, is an AI-driven video repurposing platform that automatically converts long-form video, such as podcasts, webinars, and livestreams, into short, captioned clips optimized for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts. The company was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York, United States. It has raised approximately 7 million dollars in funding to date. The product works by ingesting a long video file or recording, using AI to identify the most engaging moments (based on speech patterns, pacing, and content signals), automatically cropping and reformatting footage for vertical social platforms, generating burned-in captions, and applying branded templates so users do not need to manually edit each clip. Munch also supports content localization, generating captions and clips in more than 10 languages to help creators and brands reach international audiences without manual translation. Core features include automatic highlight detection, auto-cropping and reframing for 9:16 and other social aspect ratios, AI caption generation, trend-matching suggestions for clip styles, direct export and in some plans direct publishing to social platforms, and multi-brand workspaces aimed at agencies managing several client accounts. Pricing is tiered by monthly upload minutes: reported public plans include a Pro tier around 49 dollars per month for roughly 200 to 250 upload minutes, an Elite tier around 116 dollars per month for around 500 to 600 minutes with added features like trend-matching, and an Ultimate tier around 220 dollars per month for around 1,000 to 1,150 minutes plus priority support and early feature access, generally billed annually at these rates. The company has also expanded its offering under the Munch Studio brand into broader, done-for-you social media management, where AI generates and schedules posts from a business's website, videos, and photos, suggesting a product evolution beyond pure clip generation toward end-to-end social content operations. Munch's target audience includes podcasters, YouTubers, coaches, course creators, and marketing agencies that produce long-form video or audio regularly and need an efficient way to generate a steady stream of short-form social content without hiring a dedicated video editor. Its main competitors include Opus Clip, Vizard, Repurpose.io, Descript, and Submagic. Munch differentiates itself through its multi-language clip generation, agency-friendly multi-brand workspace, and its broader evolution into an automated, done-for-you social posting workflow rather than staying limited to clip export alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Munch is an AI video repurposing tool that turns long-form video and audio into short, captioned clips ready for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts.
Founded in 2021 and based in New York, the company has raised roughly 7 million dollars and has expanded its product line under the Munch Studio brand into broader AI-driven social media management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core workflow uses AI to detect the most engaging moments in a source video, auto-crop and reformat them for vertical social feeds, and generate burned-in captions automatically.

Additional capabilities include multi-language caption and clip generation, trend-matching style suggestions, direct export or publishing, and multi-brand workspaces for agencies managing multiple clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Munch is priced on tiered monthly plans based on how many minutes of source video are uploaded each month, starting around 49 dollars per month for the entry Pro tier.
Higher tiers, Elite and Ultimate, raise the monthly minute allowance and add features like trend-matching and priority support, with pricing generally more favorable when billed annually.

features.title: AI highlight detection
features.description: Automatically identifies the most engaging segments of long-form video for repurposing.

features.title: Auto-cropping and reframing
features.description: Reformats horizontal footage into vertical 9:16 clips suited to TikTok, Reels, and Shorts.

features.title: AI captions
features.description: Generates burned-in captions automatically without manual transcription.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Produces captions and clips in more than 10 languages for international audiences.

features.title: Trend-matching
features.description: Suggests clip styles and formats aligned with current social media trends.

features.title: Multi-brand workspaces
features.description: Lets agencies manage clip generation for multiple client brands from one account.

features.title: Direct export and publishing
features.description: Exports finished clips or publishes them directly to connected social accounts on eligible plans.

features.title: Munch Studio social management
features.description: Extends into AI-generated, done-for-you social posts built from a business's website, video, and photo assets.

pros: Significantly speeds up short-form clip production from long recordings
Multi-language caption support helps reach international audiences
Agency-friendly multi-brand workspace for managing multiple clients
Trend-matching helps clips stay aligned with current social formats
Reduces need for a dedicated manual video editor for repurposing
cons: Monthly upload minutes are capped by plan, which can limit heavy users
Pricing rises quickly at higher tiers for agencies with large video volumes
AI-selected highlights sometimes require manual review and adjustment
Product branding has shifted between GetMunch and Munch Studio, which can be confusing
Best annual-billing rates require a longer commitment than monthly plans

faqs.question: What does Munch do?
faqs.answer: Munch uses AI to automatically turn long-form video into short, captioned clips formatted for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts.

faqs.question: How much does Munch cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start around 49 dollars per month for the Pro tier with roughly 200 to 250 upload minutes, scaling up through Elite and Ultimate tiers with more minutes and features.

faqs.question: When was Munch founded?
faqs.answer: Munch was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York, United States.

faqs.question: Does Munch support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, Munch can generate captions and clips in more than 10 languages.

faqs.question: Who is Munch built for?
faqs.answer: Munch is built for podcasters, YouTubers, course creators, and marketing agencies that need to turn long recordings into short-form social content efficiently.

faqs.question: What is Munch Studio?
faqs.answer: Munch Studio is the company's expanded product line that adds AI-generated, done-for-you social media posting on top of the original video repurposing tool.

use_cases.title: Podcast to short-form clips
use_cases.description: A podcaster wants to automatically extract the best moments from each episode as short vertical clips for social promotion.

use_cases.title: Agency client content at scale
use_cases.description: A marketing agency needs to repurpose video content for several client brands without hiring separate editors for each.

use_cases.title: Multilingual audience growth

enrichment.short_description: Murf is an AI voice generator and text-to-speech platform that lets users create realistic voiceovers, dub videos, and build voice-based content using 200-plus AI voices.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium / Subscription tiers plus usage-based API pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free (10 minutes of voice generation); paid plans from about $29/user/month (Creator Lite, billed annually)
enrichment.headquarters: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States (also maintains an office in Bengaluru, India)
enrichment.seo_title: Murf AI Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Murf AI review for 2026: text-to-speech features, voice library, pricing plans, pros and cons, and the best alternatives for AI voiceovers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Murf is an AI voice generator and text-to-speech platform used to create realistic voiceovers for videos, presentations, e-learning, podcasts, and conversational applications. The company was founded in October 2020 by Sneha Roy, Ankur Edkie, and Divyanshu Pandey. Murf is headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah, and also maintains an office in Bengaluru, India, reflecting its founding team's roots. Murf has raised approximately 11.5 million dollars in total funding, including a 10 million dollar Series A led by Matrix Partners India (now Z47) with participation from Elevation Capital, completed in September 2022. The product offers more than 200 AI voices spanning many languages, accents, styles, and tonalities (such as conversational, narrative, or promotional delivery), generated through the Murf Studio web editor. Core capabilities include text-to-speech generation with adjustable pitch, speed, and emphasis, an AI voice changer that converts a user's own recorded voice into a chosen AI voice while preserving their original performance and timing, video dubbing and translation into other languages, a resume-style Studio timeline for syncing voiceover with video and music, and integrations with tools such as Canva and Google Slides. Murf also offers a developer-facing API priced on a usage basis, for example a lower-cost Falcon model billed per 1,000 characters and a higher-quality Studio-grade text-to-speech tier billed at a higher per-1,000-character rate, aimed at companies embedding voice generation into their own products. Pricing for the core Studio product follows a tiered subscription model: a Free plan offers about 10 minutes of voice generation but blocks downloads; paid tiers include Creator plans (Lite and Plus) aimed at individual creators, priced per user per month and billed favorably on an annual cycle, offering all voices, unlimited downloads, and commercial usage rights; Business plans (Lite and Plus) add team sharing, collaboration, the AI voice changer, and Windows app integration; and a custom-quoted Enterprise plan adds a Master Service Agreement, single sign-on, granular access control, a dedicated account manager, and add-ons like AI translation. Murf's target audience spans solo content creators, marketing and e-learning teams, video production studios, and enterprises that need scalable, brand-consistent voiceover production without hiring voice actors for every project. Its main competitors include ElevenLabs, Speechify, WellSaid Labs, PlayHT, and Amazon Polly. Murf differentiates itself with a polished Studio editor built around video and presentation workflows rather than raw API output alone, a large stylistic voice library, and enterprise-friendly collaboration and compliance features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Murf is an AI voice generator and text-to-speech platform that produces realistic voiceovers for video, e-learning, presentations, and podcasts using a library of more than 200 AI voices.

Founded in 2020 by Sneha Roy, Ankur Edkie, and Divyanshu Pandey, Murf is headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah, with an additional office in Bengaluru, India, and has raised about 11.5 million dollars including a Series A led by Matrix Partners India.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Murf Studio editor combines text-to-speech generation, an AI voice changer that maps a user's own recorded performance onto an AI voice, video dubbing and translation, and a timeline for syncing voiceover with video and music.
Murf also offers integrations with Canva and Google Slides plus a usage-based developer API for embedding voice generation directly into other products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Murf offers a Free plan with about 10 minutes of voice generation but no downloads, then moves into Creator Lite and Creator Plus plans priced per user per month for individual creators.

Business Lite and Business Plus plans add team collaboration and the AI voice changer, while Enterprise pricing is custom and adds SSO, an MSA, and dedicated account management; a separate usage-based API is priced per 1,000 characters.

features.title: 200-plus AI voices
features.description: Offers a large library of voices across languages, accents, and delivery styles.

features.title: AI voice changer
features.description: Converts a user's own recorded voice into a chosen AI voice while preserving timing and delivery.

features.title: Video dubbing and translation
features.description: Translates and re-voices video content into other languages.

features.title: Murf Studio timeline editor
features.description: Syncs generated voiceover with video, images, and background music in a visual timeline.

features.title: Canva and Google Slides integration
features.description: Adds AI voiceover directly into Canva designs and Google Slides presentations.

features.title: Commercial usage rights
features.description: Paid plans include rights to use generated voiceovers in commercial projects.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Business plans support shared projects, roles, and workspace management.

features.title: Usage-based developer API
features.description: Offers programmatic text-to-speech access priced per 1,000 characters for product integrations.

pros: Large, high-quality voice library with varied styles and accents
AI voice changer preserves the user's own timing and delivery
Polished Studio editor purpose-built for video and presentation workflows
Native Canva and Google Slides integrations speed up common workflows
Usage-based API option for developers building voice into their own products
cons: Free plan blocks downloads, limiting it to testing rather than real use
Per-user pricing can get expensive for larger teams
Some advanced features like the voice changer are reserved for Business-tier plans and above
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Annual billing is needed to access the lowest advertised per-month rates

faqs.question: What is Murf used for?
faqs.answer: Murf is used to generate AI voiceovers for videos, e-learning courses, presentations, podcasts, and other content using synthetic text-to-speech voices.

faqs.question: Is Murf free to use?
faqs.answer: Murf offers a free plan with about 10 minutes of voice generation, but downloads require a paid Creator, Business, or Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: How much does Murf cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at roughly 29 dollars per user per month for Creator Lite on annual billing, with Business plans and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier available for teams.

faqs.question: Who founded Murf?
faqs.answer: Murf was founded in October 2020 by Sneha Roy, Ankur Edkie, and Divyanshu Pandey.

faqs.question: Does Murf offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Murf offers a usage-based developer API priced per 1,000 characters for embedding text-to-speech into other applications.

faqs.question: What is Murf's AI voice changer?
faqs.answer: It is a feature that converts a user's own recorded voice performance into one of Murf's AI voices while keeping the original timing and delivery.

use_cases.title: Video voiceover production
use_cases.description: A content creator needs a professional-sounding narration track for a YouTube video or explainer without hiring a voice actor.

use_cases.title: E-learning course narration
use_cases.description: A training team needs consistent, scalable voiceover across many course modules in multiple languages.

use_cases.title: Product voice integration
use_cases.description: A developer wants to embed text-to-speech directly into an app or product experience using Murf's API.

enrichment.short_description: MuseScore, now branded MuseScore Studio, is a free and open-source music notation and composition application for creating, playing back, and printing sheet music.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (free) with optional premium sound packs and add-ons via MuseHub
enrichment.seo_title: MuseScore Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MuseScore Studio review for 2026: free music notation software features, Muse Sounds playback, pricing, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MuseScore, rebranded MuseScore Studio in January 2024, is a free and open-source music notation and composition program used to write, edit, play back, and print sheet music. Its origins trace to 2002, when developer Werner Schweer split notation functionality out of the MusE audio sequencer project to build a standalone notation application; the public MuseScore.org community site launched in 2008 and downloads grew quickly, reaching roughly 80,000 per month by late 2010. In 2017, the MuseScore company was acquired by Ultimate Guitar, which funded full-time paid developers alongside the existing volunteer open-source contributors. In 2021, MuseScore, Ultimate Guitar, and the audio editor Audacity were placed under a new parent holding company, Muse Group, headquartered in Limassol, Cyprus, and the MuseScore team relocated there by 2022. MuseScore Studio 4.0 launched in December 2022, introducing Muse Sounds, a proprietary sampled-instrument playback library that significantly improved audio realism compared to the previous SoundFont-based engine, along with an overhauled, more accessible interface and integration with Muse Hub, Muse Group's centralized app for managing sounds, plugins, and extensions across its product line. The core notation software supports unlimited staves with up to four voices each, dynamics, articulations, lyrics, chord symbols, lead-sheet notation, and import and export of MIDI and MusicXML 4.0 files, which lets scores move between MuseScore and commercial competitors like Sibelius, Finale, and Dorico. It also exports to PDF and WAV audio and can publish scores directly to the MuseScore.com online sharing platform. Functionality can be extended through a large community plugin repository. Companion mobile apps for iOS, Android, and Kindle Fire, available since 2014, let users browse, play back, transpose, and extract parts from shared scores, though they do not support full score creation or editing. MuseScore is translated into more than 40 languages, reflecting its large global user base of students, teachers, composers, and hobbyist musicians. The core software remains free forever with no license fee, funded instead through Muse Group's broader commercial ecosystem, including optional premium sound packs, plugins, and other paid products distributed via Muse Hub, plus MuseScore.com subscriptions for expanded sheet-music library access. MuseScore's target audience includes students and educators, self-taught musicians, composers and arrangers, and school and community music programs that cannot afford commercial notation software licenses. Its main competitors are Avid Sibelius, MakeMusic Finale, Steinberg Dorico, and Notion. MuseScore differentiates itself as the most widely used free and open-source notation tool, backed by a large active community, frequent updates, and, since MuseScore 4, professional-grade sampled playback previously found only in paid competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MuseScore, now called MuseScore Studio, is a free, open-source application for writing, editing, playing back, and printing sheet music, used widely by students, teachers, and composers.

Its roots go back to 2002, with the public MuseScore.org community launching in 2008; the project is now developed by Muse Group, headquartered in Limassol, Cyprus, alongside sibling products Audacity and Ultimate Guitar.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MuseScore supports unlimited staves and voices, dynamics, articulations, chord symbols, and lead-sheet notation, with import and export of MIDI and MusicXML for compatibility with Sibelius, Finale, and Dorico.

MuseScore Studio 4 introduced Muse Sounds, a proprietary sampled-instrument playback library, plus integration with Muse Hub for managing sounds and plugins, alongside companion mobile apps for browsing and playing shared scores.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core MuseScore Studio notation application is free and always will be, with no license fee for composing, editing, or printing scores.
Muse Group monetizes the broader ecosystem through optional premium sound packs and add-ons available in Muse Hub, plus separate paid subscriptions on the MuseScore.com sheet-music sharing platform.

cons: Some advanced engraving controls still lag behind top commercial tools like Dorico
Mobile apps only play and browse scores, they cannot create or fully edit them

faqs.question: Is MuseScore really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core MuseScore Studio notation software is free and open source, with optional paid sound packs and add-ons available separately.

faqs.question: Who created MuseScore?
faqs.answer: MuseScore originated from work by developer Werner Schweer starting in 2002, and the public MuseScore.org project launched in 2008.

faqs.question: Who owns MuseScore now?
faqs.answer: MuseScore is developed by Muse Group, a company headquartered in Limassol, Cyprus, which also owns Audacity and Ultimate Guitar.

faqs.question: What is MuseScore Studio 4?
faqs.answer: MuseScore Studio 4, released in December 2022, is the current major version, adding the Muse Sounds playback library and an updated interface.

faqs.question: Can MuseScore open Sibelius or Finale files?
faqs.answer: MuseScore can import and export MusicXML, which allows scores to move between MuseScore and other notation programs like Sibelius, Finale, and Dorico.

faqs.question: Does MuseScore have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, MuseScore has companion apps for iOS, Android, and Kindle Fire that let users play back, transpose, and extract parts from shared scores.

enrichment.short_description: musikcube is a free, open-source, cross-platform, terminal-based music player, audio engine, metadata indexer, and streaming server written in C++.
enrichment.seo_title: musikcube Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: musikcube review for 2026: this free, open-source terminal music player covers features, platforms, streaming server setup, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: musikcube is a free, open-source, cross-platform music player, audio engine, metadata indexer, and streaming server that runs primarily as a terminal (console) user interface, written in C++. It is developed and maintained principally by Casey Langen, known online as clangen, with the project hosted publicly on GitHub. The original musikcube application dates back to roughly 2004 through 2007, when an early Windows-focused version reached version 1.1; development then stalled for several years while the lead developer had professional commitments that created a conflict of interest. Around 2015, development resumed with a full rewrite focused on cross-platform support, and that modern, actively maintained codebase is what is distributed today. Because musikcube is a community open-source project rather than a company, there is no funding history, headquarters, or formal company size to report. musikcube's defining feature is its terminal-based interface, which lets users browse, search, and play a large local music library entirely from a text console, appealing to users who prefer keyboard-driven, low-resource software over graphical music players. It compiles and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is commonly deployed on low-power devices such as a Raspberry Pi, where it can double as a headless streaming audio server for a home network. A companion Android app, musikdroid, lets users stream audio from and remotely control a running musikcube instance from a phone. Under the hood, musikcube indexes a user's local music library by metadata (artist, album, genre, and so on) and exposes a redesigned plugin API that supports audio decoders and encoders, playback and play-queue manipulation, visualizers, data streams, data indexers, and digital signal processing extensions, making the player extensible by the community. Installation is available through pre-compiled binaries on the project's GitHub releases page, package managers such as Homebrew on macOS, and building from source on Linux and other platforms. musikcube's target audience is technically inclined music listeners, Linux and terminal enthusiasts, self-hosters, and Raspberry Pi hobbyists who want a lightweight, scriptable local music player and streaming server without a graphical desktop environment or a subscription streaming service. Its closest alternatives include other terminal-based or lightweight music players such as cmus, ncmpcpp paired with MPD (Music Player Daemon), and moc (Music On Console). musikcube differentiates itself by combining a terminal UI with a built-in streaming server and mobile client in a single cross-platform C++ codebase, rather than requiring users to pair a separate server daemon (like MPD) with a separate client interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: musikcube is a free, open-source music player that runs in a terminal window, combining a local audio engine, metadata indexer, and streaming server in one cross-platform C++ application.
Developed primarily by Casey Langen (clangen), the project's roots go back to 2007, with a full cross-platform rewrite that began around 2015 forming the actively maintained version used today.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: musikcube runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, including low-power devices like a Raspberry Pi, where it can act as a headless streaming audio server accessible from the musikdroid Android client.
A redesigned plugin API supports audio decoders and encoders, playback and queue manipulation, visualizers, and DSP extensions, letting the community extend the player's core functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: musikcube is entirely free and open source, distributed under an open-source license with no paid tiers or subscriptions.
Users install it via pre-compiled binaries, package managers such as Homebrew, or by building from source, at no cost beyond their own hosting hardware.

Combines player, indexer, and streaming server in a single application
Keyboard-driven terminal interface appeals to power users
Cross-platform support across Windows, macOS, and Linux
cons: Terminal interface has a learning curve for users unfamiliar with console applications
No graphical desktop UI for users who prefer visual browsing
Smaller community and slower feature cadence than mainstream music players
Documentation and setup can require some technical comfort
Mobile experience depends on the separate musikdroid app rather than a unified client

faqs.answer: musikcube is a free, open-source, terminal-based music player, audio engine, metadata indexer, and streaming server written in C++.

faqs.answer: Yes, musikcube is fully free and open source with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Who develops musikcube?
faqs.answer: musikcube is developed primarily by Casey Langen, known online as clangen, with community contributions on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can musikcube run on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, musikcube runs well on a Raspberry Pi and is often used there as a lightweight, headless streaming audio server.

faqs.question: Does musikcube have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, the companion musikdroid Android app can stream from and remotely control a running musikcube instance.

faqs.answer: musikcube compiles and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, including ARM-based devices.

enrichment.short_description: Mutagen is a fast file synchronization and network forwarding tool, now owned by Docker, that lets developers use local tools and editors while working against remote servers, VMs, and containers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (free) with a paid Mutagen Pro tier, now offered through Docker subscriptions
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); Mutagen Pro features available via paid Docker subscription plans
enrichment.company_size: Small team prior to acquisition; now part of Docker, Inc.
enrichment.seo_title: Mutagen Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mutagen review for 2026: file synchronization and network forwarding for remote development, Docker integration, pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mutagen is a file synchronization and network forwarding tool designed to let developers keep using their local editors, IDEs, and command-line tools while their code actually runs on a remote server, virtual machine, or container. It was created by software engineer Jacob Howard, who began building the project (inspired by the Unison file synchronizer, written in Go) around 2016 and later founded a company around it. Mutagen was acquired by Docker, Inc. on June 27, 2023, as part of Docker's push to improve the performance and flexibility of Docker Desktop; Docker described Mutagen as celebrated for its open-source file synchronization and networking technology enabling high-performance remote development. Mutagen's core technical capability is fast, bidirectional file synchronization: it uses low-latency filesystem watching combined with differential file transfers so that changes on a local machine propagate to a remote destination (and back) almost instantly, rather than waiting for a full directory re-copy. Users can configure synchronization behavior including sync direction, file permissions, symlink handling, and ignore patterns. Alongside file sync, Mutagen provides network forwarding, letting TCP traffic, Unix domain sockets, and Windows named pipes be tunneled between local and remote endpoints, including remote-to-remote connections and mixed transport types, which is useful for reaching databases or services running inside a remote environment as if they were local. Mutagen works across local machines, SSH-accessible servers, and Docker containers, and integrates directly with Docker Compose through configuration annotations, making it straightforward to add synchronized volumes to an existing containerized project. The open-source core of Mutagen, including its full source code, remains available on GitHub and free to use, and Docker has stated there are no plans to change the licensing of Mutagen's open-source components. A paid Mutagen Pro tier existed prior to the acquisition for additional features, and existing subscribers continued under Docker with only a billing-entity change on invoices. Since the acquisition, much of the file-synchronization functionality has been folded into Docker Desktop itself as Synchronized File Shares (available in Docker Desktop 4.27 and later), which requires a Docker Pro, Team, or Business subscription; this replaced the standalone Mutagen Extension for Docker Desktop. Mutagen's target audience is software developers and DevOps engineers who do remote or containerized development, particularly teams that want the speed of local file access with the consistency of running code on shared or cloud infrastructure. Its main alternatives and competitors include Unison (the file synchronizer that inspired it), rsync-based workflows, Docker's own bind mounts and volumes, VS Code's Remote-SSH and Dev Containers, and cloud-based development environments like GitHub Codespaces and Gitpod. Mutagen differentiates itself through its combination of high-performance bidirectional sync and flexible network forwarding in a single lightweight tool, and its now-native integration into Docker Desktop.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mutagen is a file synchronization and network forwarding tool that lets developers work locally with their preferred editors and tools while code actually executes on a remote server, VM, or container.
Created by Jacob Howard around 2016, Mutagen was acquired by Docker, Inc. in June 2023 to strengthen Docker Desktop's remote and containerized development performance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mutagen's core capability is fast, bidirectional file synchronization using low-latency filesystem watching and differential transfers, configurable for sync direction, permissions, symlinks, and ignore patterns.
It also provides network forwarding for TCP, Unix domain sockets, and Windows named pipes between local and remote endpoints, and integrates directly with Docker Compose for containerized workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mutagen's core file synchronization and forwarding tools are open source and free, with full source code available on GitHub.

A paid Mutagen Pro tier previously existed for advanced features; since the Docker acquisition, related synchronization functionality is increasingly delivered as Synchronized File Shares within paid Docker Desktop Pro, Team, and Business subscriptions.

features.title: Bidirectional file synchronization
features.description: Keeps local and remote directories in sync in near real time using filesystem watching and differential transfers.

features.title: Network forwarding
features.description: Tunnels TCP traffic, Unix sockets, and Windows named pipes between local and remote endpoints.

features.title: Docker container support
features.description: Synchronizes files and forwards network traffic to and from Docker containers.

features.title: Docker Compose integration
features.description: Adds synchronized volumes to containerized projects through configuration annotations.

features.title: SSH-based remote sync
features.description: Synchronizes files with any SSH-accessible remote server or VM.

features.title: Configurable sync behavior
features.description: Supports custom sync direction, permission handling, symlink behavior, and ignore patterns.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: Full source code available on GitHub and free to use and modify.

features.title: Docker Desktop Synchronized File Shares
features.description: Mutagen's technology now powers native high-performance file sharing inside Docker Desktop.

pros: Significantly faster file access for remote and containerized development than naive bind mounts
Open-source core remains free with full source available
Combines file sync and network forwarding in one tool
Now backed by Docker's resources and integrated into Docker Desktop
Works across SSH servers, VMs, and Docker containers
cons: Standalone Mutagen Extension for Docker Desktop has been deprecated in favor of built-in Synchronized File Shares
Advanced synchronized file sharing now requires a paid Docker Pro, Team, or Business subscription
Initial sync configuration can require some learning for complex projects
Less brand-independent since acquisition, as it is increasingly tied to the Docker ecosystem
Documentation spans both the standalone Mutagen project and Docker's own docs, which can be confusing

faqs.question: What is Mutagen used for?
faqs.answer: Mutagen is used to synchronize files and forward network traffic between a local machine and remote servers, VMs, or containers, so developers can use local tools while code runs remotely.

faqs.question: Is Mutagen free?
faqs.answer: Mutagen's core open-source file synchronization and forwarding tools are free, with source code available on GitHub; some advanced functionality is now offered through paid Docker Desktop subscriptions.

faqs.question: Who owns Mutagen now?
faqs.answer: Mutagen was acquired by Docker, Inc. in June 2023 and its technology is now integrated into Docker Desktop.

faqs.question: Who created Mutagen?
faqs.answer: Mutagen was created by software engineer Jacob Howard, with the project's origins dating to around 2016.

faqs.question: Does Mutagen work with Docker Compose?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mutagen integrates with Docker Compose through configuration annotations to add synchronized volumes to containerized projects.

faqs.question: What replaced the Mutagen Extension for Docker Desktop?
faqs.answer: The standalone Mutagen Extension was replaced by Synchronized File Shares, a native Docker Desktop feature available from version 4.27 onward.

use_cases.audience: Software developers working with remote infrastructure

use_cases.description: A team using Docker for local development needs faster file access than standard bind mounts provide, especially on macOS and Windows.

use_cases.title: Cross-environment network access
use_cases.description: A developer needs to reach a database or service running inside a remote environment as if it were running locally.
use_cases.audience: Backend engineers working across local and remote environments

enrichment.short_description: Mutiny is an AI-powered go-to-market platform that personalizes websites and generates customer-facing sales and marketing assets for B2B revenue teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom / Subscription (seat and usage-based, quote required)
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing; reported plans from around $50/seat/month (Business), Enterprise typically $25,000-plus per year
enrichment.company_size: Mid-size startup (venture-backed, headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Mutiny Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mutiny review for 2026: AI website personalization and go-to-market features, pricing, pros and cons, and the best alternatives for B2B teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mutiny is a B2B go-to-market (GTM) platform, founded in 2018 by Nikhil Mathew and Jaleh Rezaei, that originally focused on AI-powered website personalization and has since expanded into a broader AI assistant for customer-facing sales and marketing work. Mutiny is headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company has raised approximately 75 million dollars across four funding rounds from investors including Sequoia Capital, Tiger Global, Insight Partners, and Y Combinator, with a notable 50 million dollar round reported by TechCrunch in April 2022 specifically for personalizing website copy and headlines with AI. Mutiny's original and still-core product identifies companies and audience segments visiting a B2B website (using firmographic and behavioral signals, often combined with tools like Clearbit or 6sense for enrichment) and automatically personalizes headlines, testimonials, logos, calls to action, and full page experiences for each visitor segment without requiring a full site rebuild. This is aimed at account-based marketing (ABM) programs that want to convert high-value target accounts more effectively than a one-size-fits-all homepage. As of 2026, Mutiny has expanded its positioning toward a GTM AI assistant that generates a wider range of customer-facing assets beyond web pages, including personalized sales decks, landing pages, and deal rooms with built-in engagement tracking, automating tasks like account research, follow-up emails, meeting prep, and forecast reviews through integrations with a company's existing CRM and sales stack, and shipping with pre-built playbooks for stages of the buyer journey such as prospecting, evaluation, negotiation, and expansion. Mutiny does not publish self-serve pricing; it sells primarily through a sales-led, quote-based model typical of enterprise ABM and conversion-optimization software. Third-party pricing trackers report a Business plan around 50 dollars per seat per month with a credit allotment (roughly 50 credits per seat, with additional credits purchasable), and Enterprise plans starting around 30,000 dollars per year with custom credit limits, CRM integrations, single sign-on, an Account Studio feature, and a dedicated customer success manager; industry estimates put typical annual contract values in the 25,000 to 150,000-plus dollar range depending on company size and traffic volume, with separate onboarding fees reported from about 5,000 to 25,000-plus dollars. Data enrichment providers like Clearbit or 6sense are often needed alongside Mutiny for full account-identification functionality and represent an additional cost outside Mutiny's own pricing. Mutiny's target audience is B2B marketing and revenue teams, particularly demand generation, ABM, and growth marketers at mid-market and enterprise SaaS companies that have meaningful inbound website traffic and want to increase conversion rates from target accounts without engineering-heavy website changes. Its main competitors include Unbounce, Instapage, Intellimize, Optimizely, and 6sense's own on-site personalization features, as well as newer AI SDR and sales-enablement tools as it expands into that space. Mutiny differentiates itself through its no-code personalization editor built specifically for B2B marketers rather than developers, pre-built ABM playbooks, and its evolution toward a broader AI-driven GTM assistant that spans marketing and sales workflows rather than staying limited to website personalization alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mutiny is a B2B go-to-market platform that started as an AI website personalization tool for account-based marketing and has expanded into a broader assistant for generating customer-facing sales and marketing assets.

Founded in 2018 by Nikhil Mathew and Jaleh Rezaei and headquartered in San Francisco, Mutiny has raised roughly 75 million dollars from investors including Sequoia Capital, Tiger Global, and Y Combinator.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mutiny's original product identifies companies visiting a website and personalizes headlines, testimonials, logos, and calls to action for each target account segment without a full site rebuild.

Its newer GTM assistant capabilities generate personalized decks, landing pages, and deal rooms with engagement tracking, and automate account research, follow-ups, and meeting prep through CRM integrations and pre-built playbooks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mutiny does not publish self-serve pricing and sells primarily through a sales-led, custom-quote model typical of enterprise ABM software.

Third-party trackers report a Business plan around 50 dollars per seat per month with a credit allotment, and Enterprise plans starting around 30,000 dollars per year, with separate onboarding fees and possible data-enrichment costs.

features.description: Creates shared digital spaces for tracking engagement with prospects during active deals.

features.description: Reports on how target accounts interact with personalized pages and assets.

pros: No-code personalization editor is accessible to marketers without developer support
Pre-built ABM playbooks reduce setup time for common GTM motions
Backed by well-known investors and a substantial funding history
Expanding product scope covers both marketing and sales-facing workflows
Strong fit for account-based marketing programs targeting specific high-value accounts
cons: No public self-serve pricing, requiring a sales conversation to get a quote
Enterprise contracts can be expensive, often tens of thousands of dollars per year
Full functionality often requires pairing with separate data enrichment tools like Clearbit or 6sense
Onboarding fees add to the overall cost beyond the subscription itself
Best suited to companies with meaningful existing website traffic, less useful for very early-stage sites

faqs.question: What does Mutiny do?
faqs.answer: Mutiny is a B2B go-to-market platform that personalizes websites for target accounts and, more recently, generates AI-powered sales and marketing assets like decks and deal rooms.

faqs.question: How much does Mutiny cost?
faqs.answer: Mutiny does not publish self-serve pricing; reported figures show a Business plan around 50 dollars per seat per month and Enterprise plans starting around 30,000 dollars per year, plus separate onboarding fees.

faqs.question: Who founded Mutiny?
faqs.answer: Mutiny was founded in 2018 by Nikhil Mathew and Jaleh Rezaei.

faqs.question: Where is Mutiny based?
faqs.answer: Mutiny is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Mutiny require a developer to set up?
faqs.answer: No, Mutiny is built as a no-code tool so marketers can personalize website content without engineering involvement.

faqs.question: What are Mutiny's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Mutiny competes with website personalization and conversion tools such as Unbounce, Instapage, Intellimize, Optimizely, and account-based platforms like 6sense.

features: Credit-based usage (reported around 50 credits per seat)
